claim: Self-hypnosis produces measurable brain changes in the default mode network, salience network, and dorsolateral prefrontal cortex
Hypnosis for pain has large effect sizes (d=0.8-1.2) in meta-analyses of RCTs
Self-hypnosis shows comparable efficacy to therapist-guided hypnosis in several clinical domains
Gut-directed hypnotherapy is highly effective for IBS with response rates of 70-80% maintained at 5+ years
Hypnosis for anxiety shows moderate to large effect sizes across 40+ RCTs
Modern hypnosis originated from Franz Mesmer's animal magnetism, reframed by James Braid
Emile Coue's autosuggestion method directly influenced New Thought and Law of Attraction movements
The New Thought movement (Quimby, Wattles, Holmes) created the foundation for modern manifestation practices
Digital self-hypnosis apps (Reveri, Nerva) show clinical efficacy in initial trials
The sociocognitive perspective argues hypnosis effects are due to expectancy, suggestion, and role enactment rather than an altered state
Goal setting theory and implementation intentions provide evidence-based mechanisms for manifestation-like processes
Mental imagery in athletes activates similar brain regions to actual performance (functional equivalence)
Hypnosis involves reduced dorsolateral prefrontal cortex activity with increased anterior cingulate and insula connectivity
VR-augmented hypnosis shows promise for pain management beyond standard approaches
Hypnosis for weight loss produces modest but significant additional benefit over CBT alone
APA Division 30 defines hypnosis as a state of consciousness involving focused attention and reduced peripheral awareness
Approximately 10-15% of the population is highly hypnotizable, and this trait is relatively stable across the lifespan
The placebo effect and hypnosis may share overlapping neurobiological mechanisms
Hypnosis for hot flashes shows 70-80% reduction in symptoms in RCTs
Self-hypnosis protocols can be effectively delivered through mobile apps and audio recordings
Hypnosis for smoking cessation shows mixed results with effect sizes similar to other behavioral interventions
Hypnotizability is normally distributed in the population, with most people moderately suggestible
Regulation of hypnotherapy varies significantly by country, with some requiring medical licensure and others having no regulation
The cold control theory suggests hypnosis involves intentionally performing actions without conscious metacognition
Self-hypnosis is more effective when combined with regular practice and reinforcement
AI-powered personalization of hypnotic suggestions may enhance efficacy beyond standardized protocols
Hypnosis for perioperative care reduces pain, anxiety, medication use, and recovery time
The Silva Mind Control Method bridged hypnosis and the New Thought movement in the 1960s-70s
Hypnosis for pediatric patients is particularly effective due to children's high hypnotizability
Coue's law of reversed effort states that the harder one consciously tries, the less likely the desired outcome
Mental contrasting (positive fantasy + current reality) is more effective than positive thinking alone
Implementation intentions (if-then plans) double or triple goal attainment rates
Hypnosis and meditation share overlapping neural mechanisms but differ in intentionality
Nerva app shows clinically significant improvement in IBS symptoms with sustained engagement
Self-hypnosis for test anxiety significantly reduces anxiety and improves performance
Hypnosis for cancer patients reduces pain, nausea, fatigue, and distress
Self-hypnosis for sleep quality improves sleep onset and duration in RCTs
The global regulation of hypnotherapy lacks standardization, creating quality control challenges
Hypnosis for immune function shows promising but preliminary evidence
Coue's method directly influenced Norman Vincent Peale and the power of positive thinking tradition
Self-hypnosis is a trainable skill that improves with practice
The effect size of hypnosis interventions increases when combined with other evidence-based treatments
Milton Erickson's indirect/permissive approach revolutionized clinical hypnosis in the 20th century
Hypnosis for fibromyalgia shows significant pain reduction in RCTs
Reveri app by David Spiegel uses evidence-based hypnosis protocols for daily self-hypnosis
William James studied and wrote about the power of suggestion and automatic writing
Self-efficacy beliefs are strong predictors of goal attainment and behavior change
Hypnosis for dermatological conditions (warts, psoriasis, eczema) shows clinical benefit
The expectancy theory of hypnosis suggests hypnotic responses are mediated by response expectancies
Hypnosis in sport enhances focus, confidence, and physiological efficiency
